questions.prompt: What are the four operating principles of Tesla's manufacturing approach?

choices.text: First principles (not industry convention), factory-as-product, vertical integration where it controls outcomes, software-defined operations. Apply all four together; that's what's distinctive — not any single piece.

questions.explanation: The four principles work as a system; the combination is the moat. Individual pieces aren't unique — gigacasting existed in aerospace, cell-to-pack came from BYD, TPS predates Tesla — but committing to all simultaneously during product launch is.

questions.prompt: What does 'factory as product' mean operationally?

choices.text: Engineering the factory with vehicle-level rigor: spec it with performance/cost/quality targets, version it software-style, instrument every station, replace tooling when newer designs justify it, staff manufacturing engineering as elite work. The factory itself becomes a product that improves quarter over quarter.

questions.explanation: Factory-as-product means treating it as a thing that gets engineered, iterated, and measured — not as a fixed asset to amortize.

questions.prompt: When should you NOT vertically integrate?

choices.text: When the component is a commodity with strong supplier competition; when suppliers have learning curve advantages you can't catch; when capital is constrained and core product needs that capital; when demand is uncertain (fixed costs hurt downturns); when the component changes slowly. Don't burn capital on vertical integration without a strategic moat to justify it.

questions.explanation: Vertical integration is expensive and context-dependent. Copy the reasoning ('own what's strategic, buy what's commodity') not the literal Tesla playbook.

questions.prompt: A traditional plant relies on paper job sheets and operator intuition. A software-defined plant looks different how?

choices.text: Every station emits telemetry; workflows are code deployed like software releases; dashboards expose live performance; engineers iterate with a deploy/measure/keep-or-revert loop; cross-plant best-practices propagate via shared software stack. Software is the operations layer, not a tool used by operations.

questions.explanation: Software-defined manufacturing isn't 'plant with computers' — it's plant where software IS the operations layer. Workflow as code, telemetry everywhere, deploy/measure loops, cross-plant propagation.

questions.prompt: A startup making electric scooters in 2026 considers vertically integrating their battery packs 'like Tesla.' Right call?

choices.text: Probably not. The 2026 EV battery supplier ecosystem is mature; commodity packs are available cheaply. Vertical integration would burn capital with no strategic gain. Tesla's vertical battery work in 2010 was forced (no suppliers existed); 2026 is a different context. Copy Tesla's REASONING ('own what's strategic'), not the literal playbook.

questions.explanation: Vertical integration decisions are context-dependent. The Tesla 2010 context (no suppliers) is different from the 2026 context (mature ecosystem). Apply the reasoning to your context; don't copy the surface playbook.
